Keywords: first-principles, fundamental, deconstruct, rebuild, axiom, assumption-free, physics
Description: Break down a problem to its most fundamental truths and rebuild from there. Question every assumption. Separate facts from conventions.
Application to Problem Solving: Challenges conventional wisdom; Reveals hidden assumptions; Enables breakthrough solutions; Avoids reasoning by analogy alone; Forces clarity on what is actually true vs assumed
When to Use: When conventional approaches arent working; When entering a new domain; When challenging incumbents; When fundamental innovation is needed; When existing solutions feel wrong
Example: Instead of 'how do we make batteries cheaper?' (analogy to past improvement), ask 'what are the raw material costs of a battery at atomic level?' This reveals that materials cost far less than the battery, so the manufacturing process is the real cost driver.
Danger Zone: Time-consuming; Can lead to reinventing the wheel; Not every problem needs first principles; Sometimes convention exists for good reason

Keywords: inversion, backward, avoid-failure, negative, charlie-munger, reverse, pre-mortem
Description: Instead of asking how to succeed, ask how to fail and then avoid those things. Often easier to identify and prevent failure modes than to guarantee success.
Application to Problem Solving: Identifies risks and failure modes; Reveals non-obvious threats; Strengthens plans by defensive thinking; Complements forward planning; Particularly useful for risk management
When to Use: Risk assessment; Strategy development; Quality assurance; When forward thinking is stuck; When you need to challenge an optimistic plan; Pre-mortem exercises
Example: Instead of 'how do we build a great product?' ask 'how do we guarantee a terrible product?' Answers: ignore users, never test, ship with bugs, no documentation, complex UX. Then systematically prevent each.
Danger Zone: Can become overly negative; Shouldnt replace positive vision; Not all failures are preventable; Can lead to excessive caution

Keywords: second-order, consequences, ripple-effect, downstream, unintended, systems
Description: Consider the consequences of consequences. Most people think about first-order effects (what happens immediately). Second-order effects (and then what?) often determine long-term outcomes.
Application to Problem Solving: Prevents unintended consequences; Improves long-term decision making; Reveals systemic effects; Challenges short-term optimization; Identifies feedback loops and cascading effects
When to Use: Strategic decisions; Policy analysis; Organizational changes; Pricing decisions; Any decision with delayed effects; When quick fixes keep creating new problems
Example: Cutting prices (first order: more sales). Second order: competitors match, margins fall industry-wide. Third order: weaker players exit, market consolidates, survivors have more pricing power. The short-term 'win' may be a long-term loss.
Danger Zone: Hard to predict far out; Can lead to analysis paralysis; Not all second-order effects are predictable; Uncertainty compounds with each order

Keywords: competence, expertise, know-limits, stay-in-lane, delegation, knowledge-boundary
Description: Know what you know and what you dont. Operate primarily within your area of expertise. When you must go outside it, seek genuine expertise. Be honest about the boundary.
Application to Problem Solving: Prevents overconfidence in unfamiliar domains; Drives appropriate delegation; Focuses analysis where team has genuine expertise; Identifies when to bring in outside help
When to Use: Team formation; Task assignment; Self-assessment; Before making decisions in unfamiliar areas; When assembling expertise for a complex problem; Hiring decisions
Example: A technology team analyzing a market entry should recognize their circle includes technical feasibility but may exclude market dynamics, customer behavior, and regulatory landscape. They should seek partners or experts for those areas rather than guessing.
Danger Zone: Can limit ambition; Circles should expand over time; Doesnt mean never learn new things; Can be used as excuse to avoid hard work

Keywords: map-territory, model, reality, abstraction, simplification, representation, limitation
Description: Every model is a simplification of reality. Useful models capture key dynamics but always lose detail. Know the limitations of your models and check them against reality.
Application to Problem Solving: Prevents over-reliance on models; Drives reality-checking; Maintains healthy skepticism; Encourages field work and direct observation alongside analysis; Prevents 'spreadsheet strategy'
When to Use: When using financial models; When relying on frameworks; When analysis stays in the office; When models give surprisingly precise answers; When reality keeps surprising the model
Example: A perfectly calibrated market model shows linear growth. But visiting actual customers reveals emotional purchasing patterns, competitive dynamics, and regulatory risks that the model completely misses. The model is useful but incomplete.
Danger Zone: Using this as excuse to dismiss all models; Still need models to structure thinking; The point is to supplement not replace models; Some maps are much better than others

Keywords: occam, simplicity, parsimony, simple-explanation, fewest-assumptions, elegant
Description: Among competing hypotheses that explain the data, prefer the simplest one (the one with fewest assumptions). Complexity should be justified by evidence.
Application to Problem Solving: Prevents over-engineering solutions; Favors simple explanations; Reduces analysis complexity; Guides hypothesis testing (test simple hypotheses first); Prevents conspiracy theories
When to Use: When multiple explanations exist; When choosing between models; When designing solutions; When the analysis is getting overly complex; As a default until complexity is justified
Example: Customer churn increased. Complex theory: competitors launched a sophisticated loyalty program targeting our best customers using AI. Simple theory: our latest update introduced a bug making checkout fail 10% of the time. Check the simple explanation first.
Danger Zone: Simple doesnt always mean correct; Some problems are genuinely complex; Can lead to oversimplification; New evidence may require more complex explanation

Keywords: bayesian, update, prior, posterior, evidence, probability, belief-revision
Description: Start with an initial estimate (prior probability) based on what you know. Update that estimate as new evidence arrives. Good reasoning means constantly updating beliefs proportional to evidence strength.
Application to Problem Solving: Structured approach to learning from data; Prevents anchoring on initial view; Quantifies uncertainty; Enables iterative problem solving; Supports Day 1 answer methodology; Prevents all-or-nothing thinking
When to Use: Throughout analysis; When new data arrives; When testing hypotheses; When conflicting evidence emerges; When combining multiple data sources; During iterative problem solving
Example: Prior: 60% chance competitor will lower prices (based on market intelligence). New evidence: competitor hires aggressive new sales VP and files for a patent on cost reduction. Updated posterior: 80% chance of price war. Adjust strategy accordingly.
Danger Zone: Hard to set accurate priors; People under-update given evidence; Can be mathematically complex; Quality of prior matters greatly; Easy to abuse to justify any belief

Keywords: safety-margin, buffer, cushion, conservative, downside-protection, redundancy
Description: Build in a buffer for things going wrong. Dont plan for best case or even expected case alone. Leave room for error, the unknown, and bad luck.
Application to Problem Solving: Prevents catastrophic failure; Builds resilience into plans; Counteracts planning fallacy; Protects against overconfidence; Creates space for iteration; Practical risk management
When to Use: Budget and timeline planning; Capacity planning; Goal setting; Investment decisions; Any situation where downside is much worse than missing upside
Example: If your analysis says the project needs 6 months and $500K, plan for 9 months and $750K. The extra buffer isnt waste - its protection against the inevitable surprises that derail tightly planned projects.
Danger Zone: Can make projects appear unfundable; Too much buffer breeds complacency; Must be justified not arbitrary; Different situations need different margins; Team may spend buffer unnecessarily

Keywords: opportunity-cost, alternative, trade-off, best-alternative, forgone, BATNA
Description: The value of the best alternative you give up when making a choice. Every decision has an opportunity cost. Ignoring it leads to suboptimal allocation.
Application to Problem Solving: Forces consideration of alternatives; Prevents default bias; Improves resource allocation; Challenges sunk cost thinking; Makes invisible costs visible
When to Use: Resource allocation; Time management; Project prioritization; Make vs buy decisions; Strategic focus; Any choice between alternatives
Example: Spending 6 months improving an existing product (opportunity cost: not building the new product that could double revenue). The improvement project looked good in isolation but terrible when compared to the alternative.
Danger Zone: Hard to quantify; Can lead to analysis paralysis; Some opportunity costs are speculative; Not every alternative is realistic; People tend to ignore opportunity costs naturally

Keywords: regression-mean, reversion, average, outlier, normalization, statistical, random
Description: Extreme performances tend to be followed by more average performances. This is a statistical phenomenon, not a causal one. Dont over-interpret random variation.
Application to Problem Solving: Prevents over-reacting to outlier data; Calibrates expectations; Distinguishes signal from noise; Prevents false attribution of cause to random events; Important for performance evaluation
When to Use: After extreme results (good or bad); Performance evaluation; Trend analysis; When making changes based on outlier data; Sports, health, and business performance analysis
Example: A sales team has their best quarter ever. The CEO praises their new strategy. Next quarter is average. The CEO blames execution. In reality, the exceptional quarter was partly lucky, and regression to the mean was inevitable regardless of strategy.
Danger Zone: Not everything regresses; Can be used to dismiss genuine improvement; Some changes in performance are real; Important to separate signal from noise; Statistical understanding required

Keywords: leverage, intervention, high-impact, system, small-change-big-effect, acupuncture
Description: In complex systems, small changes in the right place can produce large effects. Find the leverage points where minimal effort produces maximum change.
Application to Problem Solving: System redesign; Process improvement; Organizational change; Resource-constrained environments; When looking for maximum impact with minimum effort; Identifying root causes that affect multiple symptoms
When to Use: Identify the system structure; Map feedback loops and delays; Find where small input changes create large output changes; Prioritize interventions at leverage points; Test with small experiments before scaling
Example: Instead of trying to fix 10 symptoms of employee disengagement, identify that the root leverage point is the manager-employee 1:1 meeting quality. Improving this one practice cascades into better engagement, retention, productivity, and satisfaction.
Danger Zone: Leverage points can be hard to find; May require deep system understanding; Small changes can have unexpected effects; Some leverage points are politically sensitive; System may resist intervention

Keywords: hanlon, incompetence, malice, attribution, good-faith, simplest-explanation
Description: Never attribute to malice what can be explained by incompetence, oversight, or systemic factors. Most problems come from misaligned incentives and imperfect information, not bad intentions.
Application to Problem Solving: Prevents adversarial framing; Focuses on systemic fixes not blame; Improves collaboration; Reduces conflict in team problem solving; Opens up solution space beyond punishment
When to Use: Organizational problems; Customer complaints; Partner conflicts; When things go wrong; When attributing cause to people; Team retrospectives; Conflict resolution
Example: When a department consistently misses deadlines, the first instinct is to blame the department head (malice/laziness). Hanlons Razor suggests investigating: are they understaffed? Do they have unclear priorities? Are upstream dependencies causing delays? Systemic fix beats blame.
Danger Zone: Sometimes malice IS the answer; Shouldnt be naive about incentives; Doesnt mean accept poor performance; Investigate before concluding; Some situations do involve bad actors

Keywords: network effect, platform, marketplace, viral, winner-take-all, ecosystem, flywheel, two-sided market, adoption curve
Description: The value of a product or service increases as more people use it. Creates powerful flywheels and winner-take-all dynamics. Critical for understanding platforms, marketplaces, and AI ecosystems.
Application to Problem Solving: Reveals why some problems are best solved by building platforms not products; Explains accelerating growth or decline patterns; Helps identify leverage points for market dominance; Essential for evaluating AI ecosystem and agentic marketplace strategies
When to Use: When evaluating platform vs product strategy; When analyzing competitive dynamics in tech; When a problem involves adoption curves; When building AI tools that benefit from more users/data; When vibe-coded products need distribution strategy
Example: An AI marketing tool gets better as more agencies use it (more data to train on, more templates, more integrations). This creates a flywheel where the best tool gets more users which makes it even better. Understanding this dynamic changes the problem from 'build a better tool' to 'build a better adoption loop'.
Danger Zone: Not all products have network effects; Can be confused with economies of scale; Negative network effects exist (spam, congestion); Network effects can work in reverse during decline

Keywords: power law, pareto, 80-20, fat tail, exponential, winner-take-most, concentration, outlier, vital few
Description: A small number of inputs produce the vast majority of outputs. In startups, 1% of bets create 90% of returns. In marketing, 20% of channels drive 80% of leads. Understanding power laws prevents wasting effort on the trivial many.
Application to Problem Solving: Prioritizes which problems to solve (focus on the vital few); Prevents equal allocation of resources across all issues; Explains why some solutions have 100x more impact than others; Reveals where to concentrate analytical effort
When to Use: Resource allocation decisions; Project prioritization; Marketing channel analysis; Problem triage; When you have too many problems and limited capacity; When deciding which AI projects to invest in
Example: Instead of trying to improve all 15 marketing channels equally, analyze and find that Google Ads and referrals drive 80% of qualified leads. Focus all problem-solving effort on maximizing those two channels.
Danger Zone: Not everything follows power laws; Can be used to justify neglecting important but smaller issues; The vital few may change over time; Requires data to identify the actual distribution

Keywords: compounding, compound interest, exponential, daily improvement, 1% better, snowball, accumulation, long-term
Description: Small consistent improvements accumulate exponentially over time. 1% daily improvement = 37x improvement in a year. Applies to skills, relationships, content, investments, and organizational capabilities.
Application to Problem Solving: Reveals that many problems are best solved by consistent small actions rather than dramatic interventions; Helps prioritize sustainable improvements over heroic efforts; Shows why cutting corners has long-term costs; Explains why some competitors seem to have insurmountable advantages
When to Use: Long-term planning; Habit and culture design; Content and marketing strategy; Team capability building; Health and wellness; Investment strategy; When choosing between quick fix and sustainable improvement
Example: A marketing agency that publishes one quality blog post per week for 3 years has 156 posts driving SEO traffic compounding over time. A competitor who does a burst of 20 posts then stops gets temporary traffic. Compounding beats bursting.
Danger Zone: Compounding requires patience and consistency; Not all improvements compound; Compounding can also work negatively (technical debt, relationship damage); Requires long time horizon to see results

Keywords: antifragile, resilient, chaos, disorder, uncertainty, stress-testing, robust, adaptive, skin-in-game, barbell
Description: Some systems gain from disorder and stress rather than merely surviving it. Beyond resilience (bouncing back) to antifragility (getting stronger from shocks). Design businesses, careers, and portfolios to benefit from uncertainty.
Application to Problem Solving: Reframes problems as potential sources of strength; Guides system design that improves under stress; Challenges fragile optimization in favor of robust adaptability; Essential for AI/startup strategy where failure is the primary learning mechanism
When to Use: Strategy under uncertainty; Business model design; Career planning; Portfolio construction; When the environment is volatile and unpredictable; When designing AI products that learn from errors; When building teams that grow through challenges
Example: A marketing agency that loses a major client uses it as forcing function to diversify, build recurring revenue products, and reduce client concentration risk. Post-crisis, they are stronger than before. An antifragile portfolio: 90% safe assets + 10% high-risk/high-reward AI bets — limited downside, unlimited upside.
Danger Zone: Not everything can be made antifragile; Requires willingness to experience small failures; Can be used to justify unnecessary risk; Distinguishing antifragile from reckless requires judgment

Keywords: optionality, options, flexibility, asymmetric, upside, downside, real options, cheap bets, reversible, keep doors open
Description: Preserve and create options that have limited downside but uncapped upside. Value flexibility and reversibility. Make cheap bets that give you the right but not obligation to pursue opportunities.
Application to Problem Solving: Favors reversible decisions over irreversible ones; Guides toward strategies with asymmetric payoffs; Prevents overcommitment to single paths; Makes 'exploration' a valid and valuable problem-solving strategy; Essential for AI vibe coding — build cheap MVPs to test many ideas
When to Use: When choosing between committed and exploratory strategies; For investment and resource allocation; When evaluating whether to make a decision now or preserve flexibility; When vibe coding allows rapid prototyping at low cost; Career planning and personal life decisions
Example: Instead of spending $50K and 6 months building one AI product, spend $5K vibe-coding 5 quick MVPs in 5 weeks. Each MVP is a cheap option — most will fail but you only need one to hit. The option value of learning is worth more than the sunk cost.
Danger Zone: Optionality can become an excuse for never committing; Holding too many options has a cost (attention, complexity); Some situations require bold commitment not optionality; Options expire — delay has its own cost

Keywords: lindy, time-tested, longevity, survival, proven, classic, enduring, non-perishable, track record
Description: For non-perishable things (ideas, technologies, practices, businesses), the longer something has survived, the longer it is likely to survive. Books in print for 50 years will likely be in print for another 50. A business model that has worked for 20 years has more predictive value than last months hot take.
Application to Problem Solving: Helps distinguish durable solutions from fads; Guides technology and strategy choices toward time-tested approaches; Counteracts novelty bias and FOMO; Provides a prior for predicting which practices will endure; Essential for filtering AI hype from genuine paradigm shifts
When to Use: Choosing between proven and novel approaches; Evaluating whether an AI trend has staying power; Predicting durability of business models; Making long-term career bets; When deciding between classic marketing and trendy tactics
Example: Email marketing (Lindy: 30+ years) is more likely to remain relevant than the latest social media platform (Lindy: 2 years). When evaluating AI tools: Language models have been improving for 50+ years = strong Lindy. Specific AI startups existing for 6 months = weak Lindy. Invest in the paradigm not the implementation.
Danger Zone: Applies only to non-perishable things; New innovations do sometimes genuinely replace old ones; Can be used to justify resistance to necessary change; Lindy applies to categories not specific implementations
